Scenario.title: SPACEHOP Cyber Intrusion Scenario for Water/Wastewater Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: A simulated cyber attack targeting a mid-sized water treatment facility. Complex This scenario depicts a coordinated multi-stage attack leveraging reconnaissance, resource development, and various TTPs to compromise operational technology (OT) systems, focusing on disrupting critical water management processes.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Economic espionage and disruption for demonstration purposes.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Target Artifact (Tasks), Credential Store, User Account, Windows Registry, File, OS Process, Network Traffic, Windows Service, Target Artifact (Rdp), Application Log, Algorithm, File System, Logon Session, Virtualization, Virtualization Endpoint
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Web, Phishing, Mechanism, Anonymizing Networks, TCP Header Options, Memory Corruption, Path Traversal, Cron Job, Encryption, Alternate Encoding, HTTP Request Tampering, SSH Tunnel, HTTPS, DNS Tunnel, FTP Upload

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 184, Name: Software Integrity Attack
stage.description: The SPACEHOP group utilizes AI-driven tools to analyze publicly accessible information from sources like Shodan and Google Dorks, identifying key OT systems and personnel details associated with the water treatment facility.

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 158, Name: Sniffing Network Traffic
stage.description: By deploying Reg.exe-based utilities, attackers intercept network packets to harvest credentials transmitted over unsecured channels, gaining insights into internal authentication mechanisms.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 559, Name: Orbital Jamming
stage.description: Compromised VPS instances or proxy services act as Operational Relay Boxes (ORBs), facilitating man-in-the-middle attacks and anonymizing control session establishment for subsequent operations.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 220, Name: Client-Server Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Malicious actors configure client-side environments using TOR or similar networks to mask their digital footprints, simplifying remote access setup and evasion efforts.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2024-20439: Static credential vulnerability in Cisco Smart Licensing Utility allowing unauthenticated remote access.
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 327, Name: TCP Options Probe
stage.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker exploits a static credential vulnerability discovered during reconnaissance, specifically targeting the Cisco Smart Licensing Utility exposed on internet-facing assets to gain immediate administrative access.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2012-1889: Memory corruption vulnerability in Microsoft XML Core Services enabling remote code execution.
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 164, Name: Mobile Phishing
stage.description: Simultaneously, the group employs basic spear-phishing emails directed at facility staff, aiming to deceive recipients into divulging credentials or clicking malicious links, thereby securing additional entry points.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 654, Name: Credential Prompt Impersonation
stage.description: Upon establishing initial access, attackers create persistent scheduled tasks within the Windows Registry to maintain command-and-control capabilities despite system reboots or user logouts.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226, Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Exploiting default username/password reuse common in smaller organizations, attackers create masqueraded user accounts to facilitate ongoing access and mimic legitimate operations seamlessly.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.kev: CVE-2019-10068: Failure to validate security headers in Kentico leading to unauthenticated remote code execution.
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 242, Name: Code Injection
stage.description: Using code injection techniques against vulnerable applications, likely involving manipulated Kentico web platforms, attackers escalate privileges to obtain higher-level system controls beyond their initial foothold.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 671, Name: Requirements for ASIC Functionality Maliciously Altered
stage.description: To evade monitoring tools, attackers employ process hollowing, replacing standard executable images with malicious payloads, thus obscuring their tracks and blending in with normal system activity.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 565, Name: Password Spraying
stage.description: With elevated privileges, attackers conduct password spraying across numerous user accounts, attempting common password combinations to systematically extract valid credentials for lateral movement.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 49, Name: Password Brute Forcing
stage.description: Automated brute-force attacks are launched against suspected login endpoints, particularly those protected by simplistic or outdated security measures, to uncover additional user credentials efficiently.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 413, Name: Pretexting via Tech Support
stage.description: Scanning network traffic patterns, attackers identify opportunities for internal spearphishing, crafting deceptive messages embedded in network flows to trick employees into revealing more sensitive information or initiating connections.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 550, Name: Install New Service
stage.description: Executing queries on running services, especially those with known vulnerabilities, attackers map out the facility's OT landscape, noting service versions and configurations to guide deeper exploitation phases.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 220, Name: Client-Server Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Leveraging stolen credentials via RDP, attackers move laterally across the network, compromising additional workstations or servers within the water treatment plant's infrastructure.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 202, Name: Create Malicious Client
stage.description: Establishing SSH tunnels pivoted from compromised systems, attackers navigate the network covertly, masking their movements through messaging apps and altering logs to appear as routine operations.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 80, Name: Using UTF-8 Encoding to Bypass Validation Logic
stage.description: Scraping web interfaces or accessing shared directories, attackers collect sensitive data such as SCADA configuration details or operational logs, encoding the extracted information to circumvent detection filters.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 441, Name: Malicious Logic Insertion
stage.description: Hijacking dynamic libraries during system loads, attackers intercept and copy clipboard contents containing valuable data entered manually in control room interfaces, embedding this theft within legitimate privilege escalation paths.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 216, Name: Communication Channel Manipulation
stage.description: Utilizing encrypted channels like HTTPS or implementing DNS tunneling, attackers stealthily exfiltrate large volumes of sensitive data collected throughout the campaign, disguising outbound traffic to evade network monitoring.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 552, Name: Install Rootkit
stage.description: Bypassing gatekeeper defenses, possibly through simple FTP uploads if allowed, attackers exfiltrate data by leveraging compromised relays or direct file transfers, minimizing encryption overhead for basic concealment.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645, Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Exploiting domain controller authentication mechanisms, attackers manipulate or capture Kerberos tickets to trigger unauthorized access, potentially leading to cascading failures or denial-of-service conditions within the IT and OT domains.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 480, Name: Escaping Virtualization
stage.description: Gaining control over hypervisor-level access or manipulating virtual machine configurations, attackers induce scenarios that could lead to physical safety incidents by altering real-time water treatment parameters through virtualization exploits.

Scenario.summary: This complex attack scenario demonstrates how SPACEHOP leverages a combination of reconnaissance, resource manipulation, and basic TTPs to infiltrate and disrupt water and wastewater systems, highlighting vulnerabilities in both IT and OT environments.
